body.question_1: How would you rate the technical competency of the student intern in their relevant field of study?
body.answer_1: The intern from the School of Business Administration's Human Resource Management program showed adequate technical competency, with a solid theoretical foundation that required additional scaffolding to translate effectively into professional practice. Core technical knowledge in the primary discipline areas was present and generally accurate, enabling the intern to engage meaningfully with assigned tasks after an initial onboarding period. However, familiarity with specific industry-standard tools and processes was limited, requiring time investment from senior staff to bridge the gap. Stronger emphasis on applied tool training and industry-facing projects within the curriculum would produce more immediately effective graduates and reduce the onboarding burden on host companies.

body.question_2: How well did the intern demonstrate professional communication and interpersonal skills in the workplace?
body.answer_2: This intern's professionalism was fair, showing genuine commitment and a positive attitude that formed a solid foundation, with some areas requiring coaching and development during the placement period. Reliability and punctuality were good overall, though proactive communication about progress and obstacles was inconsistent and required encouragement from supervisors. The intern engaged positively with team members and accepted feedback constructively in most situations, demonstrating the willingness to learn that is essential for professional growth. Additional preparation in formal workplace communication — particularly written reporting standards and stakeholder update practices — would improve the professional impact of graduates from this program.

body.question_3: How effectively did the intern collaborate with team members and contribute to group projects?
body.answer_3: This intern's teamwork skills were fair, with a genuine willingness to collaborate balanced by some developmental areas that became apparent as project complexity increased. The intern worked well in low-ambiguity team tasks with clear role definitions, but required additional support when navigating more complex team dynamics involving competing priorities, unclear ownership, or conflicting perspectives. Communication within the team improved noticeably over the internship duration, which is an encouraging sign of learning and adaptability. Further emphasis on collaborative project work, cross-functional team exercises, and conflict resolution skills in the Human Resource Management curriculum would produce more team-ready graduates for the professional environment.

body.question_4: How would you assess the intern's problem-solving abilities and approach to challenging or unfamiliar tasks?
body.answer_4: Problem-solving was a concerning area for this intern, representing the most significant gap between the School of Business Administration's Human Resource Management graduates and our professional workforce expectations. The intern often sought direct guidance from supervisors before attempting independent analysis or solution development, limiting their ability to work autonomously on complex assignments. When initial approaches failed, pivoting strategy independently proved difficult, and the intern sometimes became visibly discouraged rather than treating failure as diagnostic information to inform the next attempt. Building a stronger culture of structured problem-solving — through case studies, debugging exercises, and open-ended challenges — in the Human Resource Management curriculum would produce significantly more effective graduates.

body.question_5: How quickly and effectively did the intern adapt to your company's work culture, tools, and environment?
body.answer_5: The intern's adaptability to our workplace culture, processes, and evolving project requirements was superb and demonstrated a level of professional resilience and flexibility that we consistently value in permanent hires. Changes to project scope, team structure, and delivery timelines were managed with composure, and the intern made adjustments to their work approach quickly and effectively without requiring extended support or reassurance from management. They were proactive in seeking to understand the context behind changes rather than simply executing instructions, which allowed them to contribute meaningfully even in fluid project environments. This quality reflects a well-rounded education at the School of Business Administration that extends beyond technical training to genuine professional agility.

body.question_6: How proficient was the intern in English communication, both written and verbal, for professional purposes?
body.answer_6: English communication capability for this intern was reasonable, sufficient for routine internal tasks but requiring development to meet the standards expected in professional, client-facing, or international contexts. Reading comprehension of English-language materials was generally strong, enabling effective research and document processing independently. However, written composition — particularly formal reports and professional emails — required editing support, and verbal fluency in meetings was limited, particularly when discussions moved at pace or involved complex technical vocabulary. Strengthening English for Professional Purposes training in the Human Resource Management curriculum would meaningfully enhance graduates' communication readiness for the modern, increasingly international workplace.

body.question_7: How well did the intern's academic training align with the practical demands and standards of your industry?
body.answer_7: Based on our experience with this intern from the School of Business Administration's Human Resource Management program, we are impressively impressed with the alignment between the academic curriculum and the practical demands of our industry. The foundational knowledge, theoretical frameworks, and applied skills the intern brought to the role mapped closely to what we require in graduate-level professional practitioners in this field. In particular, the project-based and problem-solving components of the curriculum appear to have translated well into workplace readiness. We would encourage the School of Business Administration to continue developing its industry connections and to involve practitioners actively in curriculum review to maintain this strong alignment as the professional landscape continues to evolve.

body.question_8: What specific technical or soft skill areas do you believe the university curriculum should strengthen for future graduates?
body.answer_8: Our experience with this intern has highlighted concerning gaps between what the School of Business Administration's Human Resource Management curriculum delivers and what the professional industry actually requires from graduates in this field. Specifically, the intern lacked working proficiency in several tools and methodologies that are now industry standard, requiring us to invest significant onboarding time in areas that should be covered academically. Soft skills — particularly professional communication, project management, and stakeholder management — also appeared underdeveloped relative to what professional roles at this level require from day one. We would welcome the opportunity to engage with the School of Business Administration's curriculum design team to provide specific, actionable recommendations for improvement.

body.question_9: How would you rate the intern's overall performance and value contribution to your organization during the placement?
body.answer_9: This intern's overall performance was adequate, delivering a useful contribution to the team while also clearly indicating areas where additional academic preparation would improve professional readiness. Assigned tasks were generally completed to a functional standard, with quality improving progressively as the intern built familiarity with our tools, processes, and expectations over the placement period. The growth trajectory was positive and suggests genuine potential that a stronger academic foundation would help realize more quickly. We see this internship as a successful learning experience for the student and encourage the School of Business Administration to continue building practical readiness into its curriculum to produce even more effective graduates.

body.question_10: Based on your experience with this intern, would you consider hiring from this university in the future, and why?
body.answer_10: We would cautiously consider hiring from the School of Business Administration's Human Resource Management program again, with the expectation that the skill and readiness gaps we observed in this internship experience would be addressed through curriculum improvements in the near term. The intern's core attitude and growth potential were positive, and these qualities form a solid basis for future professional development. However, we would want to see stronger applied technical skills, better English communication preparation, and more structured professional readiness training before we significantly expand our intake from this institution. We encourage the School of Business Administration to reach out to us directly to establish a closer industry-academia collaboration that benefits both future interns and our organization's talent development strategy.
